coredata.dc:title: Photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity by regulating the pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of cationic dye on carbon-based photoactive material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Rong
Wu, Wenying
Yang, Ziyan
Deng, Wenfang
Tan, Yueming
Xie, Qingji
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity by regulating the pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of cationic dye on carbon-based photoactive material. A composite material of polyetherimide (PEI) coated carbon nitride (C3N4)-carbon quantum dots (CDs) is prepared and proposed as a cathodic photoactive material for PEC assay. The surface charge of C3N4-CDs@PEI strongly depends on the solution pH, so the electrostatic adsorption of cationic methylene blue (MB) on C3N4-CDs@PEI and MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be facilely regulated by varying the solution pH. Because the solution pH can be regulated by urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, the MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be utilized for sensitive photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity with a broad linear range (0.5–100 U L−1) and ultralow detection limit (0.16 U L−1). Moreover, the proposed PEC assay platform has high selectivity and good stability, and can be used for detecting urease activity in human saliva samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocurrent polarity switchable assay
Urease activity
Electrostatic adsorption
Carbon-based photoactive material
Cationic dye

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and PEC study of C3N4-CDs
Characterization and PEC study of C3N4-CDs@PEI
pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of MB on C3N4-CDs@PEI and MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI
Photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305041 and 22474035), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2024JJ5253), Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (23A0055), and Innovation Project of College Student (S202410542213).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (inset of a) size distribution of CDs. (b) TEM and (inset of b) HRTEM images of C3N4-CDs. (c) TEM image of C3N4. (d) XPS survey spectra of C3N4 and C3N4-CDs. (e) High-resolution C 1s peaks of C3N4 and C3N4-CDs. (f) High-resolution N 1 s peaks of C3N4 and C3N4-CDs.
(a) Photocurrent curves of CDs/ITO, C3N4/ITO, and C3N4-CDs/ITO. (b) Illustration of the charge transfer mechanism of C3N4-CDs/ITO.

(a) FTIR spectra of C3N4-CDs, PEI, and C3N4-CDs@PEI. (b) Apparent zeta potential curves of C3N4-CDs@PEI at different solution pH. (c) Apparent zeta potentials of C3N4-CDs@PEI as functions of solution pH. (d) Photocurrent curves of C3N4-CDs/ITO and C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO.

(a) MB adsorption capacity of C3N4-CDs@PEI at different solution pH. (b) Photocurrent curves and (c) transient photocurrents of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption at different solution pH. (d) Illustration of the charge transfer mechanism of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption.

(a) Photocurrent curves and (b) calibration curve for PEC assay of urease activity. (c) Stability and (d) selectivity for PEC assay of 10 U L−1 urease. 100 U mL−1 GOx, 100 U mL−1 ALP, 10 mg mL−1 Glu, 10 mg mL−1 UA, 10 mg mL−1 Leu, 10 mg mL−1 Gly, 10 mg mL−1 L-Ser, 20 μM Asp, 2 μg mL−1 Lyz, 100 μM Lys, 30 μM His, and 100 μg mL−1 α-Amy are used in selectivity measurements.
Illustration of (a) the synthesis of C3N4-CDs@PEI and (b) the principle for photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity.

head.ce:title: Photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity by regulating the pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of cationic dye on carbon-based photoactive material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity by regulating the pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of cationic dye on carbon-based photoactive material. A composite material of polyetherimide (PEI) coated carbon nitride (C3N4)-carbon quantum dots (CDs) is prepared and proposed as a cathodic photoactive material for PEC assay. The surface charge of C3N4-CDs@PEI strongly depends on the solution pH, so the electrostatic adsorption of cationic methylene blue (MB) on C3N4-CDs@PEI and MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be facilely regulated by varying the solution pH. Because the solution pH can be regulated by urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, the MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be utilized for sensitive photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity with a broad linear range (0.5–100 U L−1) and ultralow detection limit (0.16 U L−1). Moreover, the proposed PEC assay platform has high selectivity and good stability, and can be used for detecting urease activity in human saliva samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a nickel-containing enzyme, urease catalyzes the hydrolysis of urea to produce ammonia and thus increases the solution pH, which has attracted widespread attention from scientists in the fields of biomedicine and agriculture [1]. In organisms, urease plays a crucial role in a variety of metabolisms, and urease activity is associated with many urinary and gastrointestinal diseases [2]. For early disease diagnosis, urease activity in the oral cavity has been considered to be an important biomarker [3]. Several methods including fluorescence, colorimetry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, and electrochemistry, have been used to detect urease activity [4–13]. However, these methods still suffer from complex operations, long analysis time, and expensive instruments. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop a simple and effective method for sensitive and selective detection of urease activity.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis has been considered to be a novel and promising analytical technique because of its high sensitivity, simple operation, low cost, and easy miniaturization [14–21]. PEC analysis is classified into anodic analysis, cathodic analysis, and photocurrent polarity switchable analysis according to the photocurrent polarity. Because of the improved accuracy and anti-interference capability, photocurrent polarity switchable analysis has drawn special attention [22–26]. The improved accuracy of photocurrent polarity switchable analysis results from the opposite photocurrent polarities in the presence and absence of the analyte [27–29]. In photocurrent polarity switchable analysis, the improved anti-interference capability is attributed to the distinct photocurrent polarities in the presence of the analyte and interferents, respectively [30–32]. As far as we are aware, photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity has not been reported up to now. In view of the great advantages of photocurrent polarity switchable analysis, the development of a novel strategy for photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity is significant. The reversal of photocurrent polarity can be accomplished through three primary approaches: (1) incorporation of additional semiconductors or active small molecules, (2) modulation of excitation light wavelength, and (3) application of variable bias potentials [22–32]. Notably, the integration of additional semiconductors or active small molecules through target recognition (e.g. antigen–antibody recognition) emerges as the predominant methodology for achieving controllable photocurrent polarity reversal [30,33]. To broaden the analytical applications of photocurrent polarity switchable analysis, it is desirable to establish innovative polarity-switching strategies. Urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea can enable precise solution pH control and further regulate the electrostatic adsorption of active small molecules on photoactive materials, which may provide a novel photocurrent polarity switching strategy.

Herein, a composite material of polyetherimide (PEI) coated carbon nitride (C3N4)-carbon quantum dots (CDs) is prepared and proposed as the photoactive material for PEC assay (Scheme 1
a), which outputs an excellent cathodic photocurrent. It is found that the negative surface charge of C3N4-CDs@PEI changes to a positive surface charge when the solution pH increases. At low solution pH, the adsorption of methylene blue (MB) on C3N4-CDs@PEI is inhibited because of the electrostatic repulsion between the cationic dye (MB) and positively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI, whereas the electrostatic attraction between MB and negatively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI at high solution pH can increase the MB adsorption capacity of C3N4-CDs@PEI and thereby switch the photocurrent polarity. The solution pH can be regulated by urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, so the MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be utilized for PEC assay of urease activity (Scheme 1 b). The present work not only reports a novel carbon-based cathodic photoactive material but also develops a novel strategy for photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity.

For the preparation of CDs, a solution was prepared by dissolving C2H8N2 (335 µL) and C6H8O7 (1.05 g) in of ultrapure water (10 mL) and then hydrothermally heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After centrifugation and purification, the obtained CDs were dried in a freeze dryer.

For the preparation of C3N4-CDs, CO (NH2)2 (3 g) was mixed with CDs (10 mg) in ultrapure water (20 mL), followed by freeze-drying. The mixture was placed in a covered crucible, then heated to 550 ℃ at 0.5 ℃ min−1, and finally maintained at 550 ℃ for 3 h. C3N4 was also prepared similarly to C3N4-CDs except for the absence of CDs.

For the preparation of C3N4-CDs@PEI, 2 mL of 0.10 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) containing 1 wt% PEI (MW 600 Da) was added into 10 mL of PBS containing 2 mg mL−1 C3N4-CDs. The mixture was continuously stirred for 1 h, centrifuged, washed, and finally dried in a freeze dryer.

20 μL of well dispersed C3N4-CDs@PEI (2 mg mL−1) solution was dropped on a clean indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode (0.25 cm2), and dried at room temperature for later use. 50 μL of CO(NH2)2 aqueous solution (20 mM) was mixed with 90 μL of acetate buffer solution (5 μM, pH 4.0) and 100 μL urease aqueous solution of a certain concentration (0–100 U L−1) in 96 well plates. After the reaction at 37 °C for 30 min, 10 μL of MB aqueous solution (10 μM) was added to the above 96 well plates, and the C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO electrode was soaked in the above solution for 15 min. After washing the electrode three times with PBS, the C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO electrode after MB adsorption was used as the working electrode for the PEC test in the three-electrode system. PEC test was carried out at 0.05 V (vs. SCE) in 0.10 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.10 M ascorbic acid (AA). For PEC detection of urease activity in human saliva samples, 10.0, 20.0, and 50.0 U L−1 urease standard solutions were spiked in the human saliva samples, and other procedures for PEC detection of urease activity in human saliva samples were similar to those in acetate buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and PEC study of C3N4-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As illustrated in Scheme 1 a, CDs were prepared from ethylenediamine and citric acid by a hydrothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (SEM) image shows that CDs have a spherical morphology with a mean diameter of 2.0 nm (Fig. 1
a). CDs show a high ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption peak at 346 nm ( Fig. S1
a) and an optimal fluorescence emission wavelength at 445 nm ( Fig. S1
b). These observations confirm the successful synthesis of CDs.

The as-prepared CDs were mixed with urea and heated at 550 °C for 3 h to prepare C3N4-CDs. For comparison, C3N4 nanosheets were also prepared similarly to C3N4-CDs except for the absence of CDs. The TEM image of C3N4-CDs shows that a large amount of ultrasmall CDs are distributed on the surface of C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 1 b). In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of C3N4-CDs (inset of Fig. 1 b), a lattice spacing of 0.203 nm is observed, consistent with the (101) planes of graphitic carbon. In stark contrast to the relatively rough surface of C3N4-CDs, C3N4 nanosheets possess a relatively smooth surface (Fig. 1 c). CDs show a broad X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak at 23.5°, whereas C3N4 nanosheets show (100) diffraction peak at 13.0° and (002) diffraction peak at 27.4° ( Fig. S2 ). The diffraction peaks of C3N4 are also observed in the XRD pattern of C3N4-CDs, suggesting that the presence of CDs does not affect the crystal structure of C3N4. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) result of C3N4 indicates that the C/N atomic ratio is close to the theoretical value of 3:4 (Fig. 1 d), confirming the successful synthesis of C3N4. The high-resolution C 1s peak of C3N4 shows a C=C peak at 284.7 eV and an N–C=N peak at 288.2 eV (Fig. 1 e). Besides these two peaks of C3N4, two additional small peaks are also observed in the high-resolution C 1s peak of C3N4-CDs at 285.8 and 288.9 eV (Fig. 1 e), which are attributed to C–OH and COOH groups of CDs, respectively [34]. The N 1s spectra of both C3N4 and C3N4-CDs show typical C-N=C peak at 398.5 eV, N-(C)3 peak at 400.1 eV, and C–N–H peak at 401.5 eV (Fig. 1 f) [35]. Interestingly, a new peak appears at 397.7 eV in the N 1s spectrum of C3N4-CDs, probably due to the interaction between CDs and N atoms in C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 1 f) [36]. These results demonstrate the successful preparation of C3N4-CDs.

The PEC properties of CDs, C3N4, and C3N4-CDs were studied at 0.05 V (vs SCE) in 0.10 M phosphate PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.10 M AA. The C3N4/ITO shows an anodic photocurrent of 10 nA under the intermittent visible-light irradiation from 10 to 20 s, whereas the CDs/ITO shows a cathodic photocurrent of 25 nA (Fig. 2
a). Interestingly, the cathodic photocurrent of C3N4-CDs/ITO is as high as 155 nA, which is 6.2-fold that of CDs/ITO. The above results prove that C3N4 owns a significant sensitization effect on CDs.

To understand the improved cathodic photocurrent of C3N4-CDs/ITO, the energy levels of CDs and C3N4 were tested. In the whole UV–vis light region, CDs show good light absorption, whereas C3N4 has excellent visible light absorption below 580 nm ( Fig. S3
a). The relatively narrow band gaps (E g) of CDs and C3N4 are 2.0 and 2.81 eV, respectively ( Fig. S3
b). The valence band edges (E VB) of CDs and C3N4 are estimated to be 1.51 and 1.38 eV based on the valance band XPS spectra of CDs and C3N4 ( Fig. S3
c), respectively. Therefore, the conduction band edges (E CB) of CDs and C3N4 are calculated to be −0.49 and −1.43 eV based on the formula E CB = E VB − E g, respectively. To study the proposed charge transfer mechanism of C3N4-CDs, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was conducted using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent ( Fig. S4 ). C3N4-CDs can generate ·O2 – but no ·OH under visible-light irradiation. The E CB of CDs lies below the O2/·O2 – redox potential (−0.33 V vs NHE), providing the necessary driving force for single-electron oxygen reduction to generate ·O2 –. The E V B of C3N4 is insufficiently positive to drive water oxidation (2.27 V vs NHE for ·OH generation). The selective radical generation, together with the experimentally measured energy level levels of CDs and C3N4 indicates the formation of a type II heterojunction in the C3N4-CDs nanocomposite. The charge transfer mechanism of C3N4-CDs/ITO is depicted in Fig. 2 b. Because C3N4 has a more negative E CB value than CDs, the visible-light excited photoelectrons will transfer from C3N4 to CDs and then be used for oxygen reduction. Because of the photoelectron transfer from C3N4 to CDs, more photoelectrons are used for oxygen reduction, resulting in the superior cathodic photocurrent of C3N4-CDs to that of CDs alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and PEC study of C3N4-CDs@PEI
ce:section.ce:para:
To facilitate the adjustment of the surface charge of C3N4-CDs at different solution pH, PEI was coated on C3N4-CDs. PEI can form a stable coating on C3N4-CDs through multiple interactions: (i) electrostatic attraction between protonated amine groups (–NH3 +) and surface carboxylates (–COO−) of C3N4-CDs, (ii) hydrogen bonding with surface –OH/–NH groups, and (iii) hydrophobic interactions between alkyl chains of PEI and sp2-hybridized carbon domains. Apparent zeta potentials of C3N4-CDs before and after PEI coating were measured ( Fig. S5 ). After PEI coating, the apparent zeta potential changes from −45.5 to −36.5 mV. The decreased negative surface confirms the coating of PEI on C3N4-CDs. The as-prepared C3N4-CDs@PEI was further characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 3
a). C3N4-CDs show the stretching vibrations of COOH at 3200 cm−1, C=N and C-N heterocycles between 1200 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, and the triazine ring at 811 cm−1, whereas PEI shows the stretching vibrations of N–H at 3410 cm−1, –CH2– at 2920 and 2850 cm−1, and C-N at 1118 cm−1. In the FTIR spectra of C3N4-CDs@PEI, all characteristic peaks of both PEI and C3N4-CDs are observed, confirming the successful preparation of C3N4-CDs@PEI.

The surface charges of C3N4-CDs@PEI at different solution pH were measured (Fig. 3 b). At pH 3.0 and 4.0, the apparent zeta potentials of C3N4-CDs@PEI are 40.8 and 24.7 mV, respectively, suggesting the positively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI surfaces at low solution pH. At pH 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, and 9.0, the apparent zeta potentials of C3N4-CDs@PEI are −6.5, 26.2, −36.7, −42.6, and −47.9 mV, respectively, suggesting the negatively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI surfaces at relatively high solution pH. With the increase of solution pH, the negative surface charge of C3N4-CDs@PEI increases accordingly, and the zero charge point occurs at pH 4.8 (Fig. 3 c). As confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy, there are abundant –COOH and –NH2 groups in C3N4-CDs@PEI, so the surface charge of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be facilely tailored by varying the solution pH. The transient photocurrent of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO is comparable to that of C3N4-CDs/ITO (Fig. 3 d), suggesting that PEI coating does not severely affect the PEC property of C3N4-CDs. In addition, the photocurrent curves and electrochemical impedance spectra of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after the incubation in aqueous solutions of different pH for 60 min show no obvious change ( Fig. S6 ), suggesting that C3N4-CDs@PEI has good stability at different solution pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of MB on C3N4-CDs@PEI and MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption ability of C3N4-CDs@PEI for cationic MB was measured at different solution pH ( Fig. S7 ). At pH lower than 4.0, the MB adsorption capacity of C3N4-CDs@PEI is extremely low because of the electrostatic repulsion between the cationic MB and positively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI (Fig. 4
a). When the solution pH increases from 5.0 to 9.0, the electrostatic attraction between MB and negatively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI becomes stronger, so the MB adsorption capacity of C3N4-CDs@PEI increases accordingly (Fig. 4 a).

The PEC performance of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption at different solution pH was measured (Fig. 4 b). The C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption at pH 3.0 and 4.0 still shows cathodic photocurrents, because the adsorption of MB on C3N4-CDs@PEI at low solution pH is inhibited because of the electrostatic repulsion between MB and positively charged C3N4-CDs@PEI. The C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption at solution pH from 5.0 to 9.0 shows anodic photocurrents because the adsorption of MB on C3N4-CDs@PEI can switch the photocurrent polarity. The MB adsorption capacity of C3N4-CDs@PEI increases with the increase of solution pH, so the anodic photocurrents of the resulted photoelectrodes increase accordingly (Fig. 4 c). The photocurrent curves of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after the incubation in aqueous solutions (without MB) of different pH for 60 min show comparable cathodic photocurrents ( Fig. S6
a), confirming that the reversal of photocurrent polarity is attributed to MB adsorption on C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO. Transient photocurrents of MB-adsorbed C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after being washed by 0.10 M PBS (pH 7.4) for different times are comparable ( Fig. S8 ), suggesting the MB molecules adsorbed on C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO cannot be removed by PBS washing. To achieve the high photocurrent response, the concentration of C3N4-CDs@PEI solution for the preparation of C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO was optimized ( Fig. S9 ). The C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO electrode prepared by casting 20 μL of C3N4-CDs@PEI solution (2 mg mL−1) on the ITO electrodes shows the highest photocurrent response. Therefore, 2 mg mL−1 C3N4-CDs@PEI solution was used for the preparation of the photoelectrode.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) are reported to be 1.61 and −0.25 eV, respectively [33]. As schematically shown in Fig. 4 d, because the E CB values of both C3N4 and CDs are more negative than the LUMO of MB, the photogenerated holes transfer from MB to CDs and then to C3N4, which are finally scavenged by AA. Because the HOMO of MB is more positive than the E VB values of both C3N4 and CDs, the photoelectrons transfer from C3N4 to CDs, then to MB, and finally to the ITO, so an anodic photocurrent is generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The solution pH can be regulated by urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, so the MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI can be utilized for PEC assay of urease activity. Experimentally, urease was used to catalyze the hydrolysis of urea in MB aqueous solution (initial pH 4.0) to vary the solution. Then the C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO photoelectrode was soaked in the resulted MB solution, and the photoelectrode after MB adsorption was subjected to PEC measurement. The C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption at pH below 4.0 outputs cathodic photocurrent, and the C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO after MB adsorption at pH above 4.0 output anodic photocurrent (Fig. 4 b), so the initial pH of MB aqueous solution is selected to be 4.0. At bias potentials lower than 0.05 V, photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity can be achieved ( Fig. S10
a). To obtain the highest anodic photocurrent response for photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity, a bias potential of 0.05 V was used for PEC measurement. To obtain the highest sensitivity for PEC assay of urease activity, the time for urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea is optimized to be 30 min ( Fig. S10
b), the MB concentration is optimized to be 10 μM ( Fig. S10
c), and the time for MB adsorption on the C3N4-CDs@PEI/ITO photoelectrode is optimized to 15 min ( Fig. S10
d).

Under optimized conditions, a photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity was carried out (Fig. 5
a). The photoelectrode outputs a cathodic photocurrent in the absence of urease, whereas the photoelectrode outputs cathodic photocurrents in the presence of different concentrations of urease. Urease can catalyze the hydrolysis of urea to increase the solution pH, and thereby MB can adsorb on the photoelectrode through electrostatic attraction, resulting in the photocurrent polarity switching of the photoelectrode. With the increase in urease activity, the anodic photocurrent increases. As shown in Fig. 5 b, there is a linear correlation between photocurrent change (Δi, nA) and the logarithm of urease activity (log c, U L−1) from 0.5 to 100 U L−1, with a regression equation is Δi = 331log c + 269 (R 2 = 0.9981). At the signal-to-noise ratio of 3, the detection limit is estimated to be 0.16 U L−1, which is better than the reported values ( Table S1 ). The relative standard deviations (RSDs) are lower than 5.1 % for three independent assays at each urease activity, suggesting an acceptable repeatability.

For the PEC assay of 10 U L−1 urease, the photoelectrode can maintain 91.7 % of its initial photocurrent response after 15 intermittent illumination cycles (Fig. 5 c), suggesting that the photoelectrode has acceptable stability for the PEC assay of urease activity. Some interferents including glucose oxidase (GOx), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), glucose (Glu), uric acid (UA), leucine (Leu), glycine (Gly), L-serine (Ser), aspartic acid (Asp), lysozyme (Lyz), lysine (Lys), histidine (His), and α-amylase (α-Amy) cannot switch the photocurrent polarity. The photoelectrode still outputs a cathodic photocurrent (Fig. 5 d). By contrast, only urease can switch the photocurrent polarity, and an anodic photocurrent is observed. Moreover, comparable photocurrent responses are achieved for urease alone and a mixture containing urease and all interferents, suggesting good selectivity for PEC assay of urease activity. Urease is a crucial diagnostic marker for Helicobacter pylori, and the detection of urease activity has been considered to be a reliable non-invasive method for both initial H. pylori screening and therapeutic follow-up [8,37]. Therefore, the PEC assay of urease activity was conducted in human saliva samples by the standard addition method ( Table S2 ). The recoveries are achieved between 94.4 % and 105 %, and the RSDs are below 4.8 %, indicating the feasibility of our method for photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity in real samples.

In summary, photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity is developed by regulating the pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of MB on C3N4-CDs@PEI photoactive material. The surface charge of C3N4-CDs@PEI strongly depends on the solution pH, so the electrostatic adsorption of MB on C3N4-CDs@PEI and MB-triggered photocurrent polarity switching of C3N4-CDs@PEI are facilely regulated by varying the solution pH. By varying the solution pH through urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, a sensitive and selective photocurrent polarity switchable assay of urease activity is realized. This work has provided a novel photocurrent polarity switching strategy by regulating the pH-dependent electrostatic adsorption of cationic dye on carbon-based photoactive material and also developed a simple and effective method for detecting urease activity.

Rong Liao: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenying Wu: Data curation, Conceptualization. Ziyan Yang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenfang Deng: Writing – review & editing. Yueming Tan: Writing – review & editing. Qingji Xie: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305041 and 22474035), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2024JJ5253), Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (23A0055), and Innovation Project of College Student (S202410542213).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114202.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The action of urease in the decomposition of urea

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Mack, D.S. Villars, The action of urease in the decomposition of urea, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 45 (1923) 505-510.
